title: Chris Boardman says fuel price surge a reason to swap car for bike

input.lead: Olympic medal winning cyclist Chris Boardman calls on drivers to change the way they travel
input.summary: Olympic medal winning cyclist Chris Boardman calls on drivers to change the way they travel
input.evidence_text: The average price of unleaded petrol has risen to 158.52p a litre according to the RAC, with further increases predicted Boardman, whose job as England's Active Travel Commissioner is to encourage walking and cycling, said switching modes of transport would help families save "thousands of pounds a year" He told BBC Radio 5 Live Breakfast : "You need moments in life where there's a reason to try something different and the high fuel costs at the moment are exactly that." According to the latest government figures, 59% of all journeys in the UK are made by a car or van while 2% are on a bike Boardman, from Wirral on Merseyside, said the situation could see the UK mirror the changes made in the Netherlands more than fifty years ago The OPEC oil crisis in the 1970s, coupled with a spike in child road deaths led the Netherlands to shift from a broadly car-centric country to one that favoured the bicycle

title: White Hotel club in Salford to close for good

input.lead: After more than a decade of raves, DJ sets, Salford's experimental club is closing, owners confirm
input.summary: After more than a decade of raves, DJ sets, Salford's experimental club is closing, owners confirm
input.evidence_text: After more than a decade hosting experimental electronic and techno music, out of a white-brick garage - avant-garde venue The White Hotel, in Salford, said it will be shutting its doors for good in January 2027 Famed for its underground culture and its eclectic range of artists - the club is now in a flood-risk zone, according to its owners - who said they decided they wanted to leave on their own terms "We've had a hell of a run these past 10 years," The White Hotel told the BBC. "But Rome never got to decide when it fell, we're getting to execute our own demise. The time just feels right for that "The team's energies are firmly on this final march to January in order to make it as magical as possible," The White Hotel said "Our rebirth is coming long before we close in the form of our first festival, The Black Lights, taking the very best of what The White Hotel does to the Lancashire coast, for a three day spectacle in Blackpool," the club added In Greater Manchester you are spoilt for choice of places to enjoy live music

title: Wilmslow twins do Great Manchester Run for Down's Syndrome funds

input.lead: Harry and Thomas Derricott, 24, will be raising money for the charity Down's Syndrome Manchester
input.summary: Harry and Thomas Derricott, 24, will be raising money for the charity Down's Syndrome Manchester
input.evidence_text: Twins Thomas and Harry Derricott, from Wilmslow, Cheshire, are part of a group of 10 young adults with the condition who will be running together, along with 30 friends and family members They are raising money for the charity Down's Syndrome Manchester, with £18,000 donated so far "I'm excited to do the 10k for my first time," Harry said, while Thomas added he could "not wait" to take part in his second such run "I can't wait to do the 10k again," he said, adding: "It's my second time and this year I'm doing it with my twin brother, my good friends and our friends and Greg and Jack from high school." The 24-year-olds said their training had included going to a gym and doing their local Park Run every weekend "We're all ready - we've been doing lots of training, lots of walking and healthy eating," their mum Jill Perry told BBC Radio Manchester

title: Two arrested as police raid Manchester illegal gambling den

input.lead: Police say illegal gambling can cause many issues and it is "important" such dens are shut down
input.summary: Police say illegal gambling can cause many issues and it is "important" such dens are shut down
input.evidence_text: Police found poker tables, gambling chips, alcohol, cash and account books at premises in Crown Street, Manchester, on Thursday A 33-year-old man and a woman, aged 66, were arrested on suspicion of two offences under the Gambling Act 2005 and Licensing Act 2003 respectively PC Nial Vivian of Greater Manchester Police, said: "Illegal gambling can cause many issues, from disputes about cash to money laundering, and it is important we shut them down whenever we come across them." title: 'We need integration so Oldham 2001 riots don't happen again'

input.lead: The man charged with bridging racial divides after the violence in 2001 calls for deeper integration
input.summary: The man charged with bridging racial divides after the violence in 2001 calls for deeper integration
input.evidence_text: On 26 May 2001, tensions between white and Asian young people in Oldham erupted in three days of violence, which later spread to Burnley and crossed the Pennines to Bradford Prof Ted Cantle was commissioned by the then Home Secretary David Blunkett to lead a review into what caused the violence in the aftermath Cantle said while some of the latent racism that existed in 2001 had disappeared, "the parallel lives that I described 25 years ago definitely still exist" "They've got a little bit better in one or two places and a little bit worse in others," he said In his report, Cantle highlighted deep-rooted segregation between ethnic groups leading what he described as "parallel lives" where those from different backgrounds did not mix He advised the government and councils at the time to ensure schools were less segregated

title: Manchester University work placement plans offer students 'hope'

input.lead: The university says the plans would give graduates a better chance in the job market
input.summary: The university says the plans would give graduates a better chance in the job market
input.evidence_text: Duncan Ivison, vice chancellor of the University of Manchester, has announced plans to offer students industry experience or learning partnerships during the course of their degrees He said while the plans were ambitious they had "no choice" but to do this to give students a better chance in the job market after graduation, amid increasing challenges such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) He said the plans would involve investment and building a team of colleagues to support more than 30,000 students throughout the process The initiative includes not just placements but other opportunities such as projects between local, national and global partners such as projects that involve students having to use their skills Prof Ivison said: "You've got a very difficult labour market, you've got a very volatile political situation, you've got AI transforming people's lives in all kinds of ways, tough economy "I think we need to give some hope and aspiration to young people as much as anything else."

title: No second retrial for men accused of Manchester Airport brawl

input.lead: Muhammad Amaad and Mohammed Fahir Amaaz denied assaulting a Greater Manchester Police officer in 2024
input.summary: Muhammad Amaad and Mohammed Fahir Amaaz denied assaulting a Greater Manchester Police officer in 2024
input.evidence_text: The Crown Prosecution Service said it would not be ordering a further hearing for Muhammad Amaad, 26, and Mohammed Fahir Amaaz, 21, after two previous juries failed to reach verdicts at Liverpool Crown Court The pair had denied assaulting Greater Manchester Police constable Zachary Marsden near the car park area of Terminal 2 on 23 July 2024 Amaaz was previously convicted of assaulting two female officers, PC Lydia Ward and PC Ellie Cook, and a Starbucks customer during the same alleged incident. He will be sentenced on 26 June Amaaz appeared via videolink from custody while Amaad sat with his lawyers in the court as prosecutor Paul Greaney KC outlined the reasoning for not applying for a second retrial He said the law was clear in that there would be a "presumption and expectation" that there are no third trials save for "exceptional circumstances" Judge Neil Flewitt KC directed that verdicts of not guilty be recorded against the two defendants

title: Man who sprayed bottle of semen on women in Cheshire jailed

input.lead: William Kidd was jailed for four years in a hearing at Manchester Minshull Street Crown Court
input.summary: William Kidd was jailed for four years in a hearing at Manchester Minshull Street Crown Court
input.evidence_text: William Kidd, 27, was convicted of 25 offences and sentenced to four years imprisonment in a hearing at Manchester Minshull Street Crown Court At a previous hearing, he pleaded not guilty to 11 counts of sexual assault, eight counts of filming women underneath their clothing and one count of possession of cannabis - but he changed his pleas to guilty at the hearing on Friday He previously pleaded guilty to five counts of voyeurism The court heard Kidd, of no fixed abode, committed a number of offences against women in shops in the Macclesfield and Handforth areas of Cheshire and the Denton area of Greater Manchester from May to September 2025 He was caught on CCTV following a number of women around various shops before spraying liquid from a bottle onto them, without their knowledge. He was also recorded filming some women from behind, underneath their clothing When asked why he was spraying the liquid at women, he told one security guard, "I'm a sex addict", and another, "I did it to get a sexual thrill"

title: 'If my dad was alive he'd be laughing his head off going 'yes, get in!''

input.lead: Salford 'If my dad was alive he'd be laughing his head off going 'yes, get in!'' Teacher Belinda Neild took up running after the death of her mum on the eve of her wedding day.
input.summary: Salford 'If my dad was alive he'd be laughing his head off going 'yes, get in!'' Teacher Belinda Neild took up running after the death of her mum on the eve of her wedding day. She has since completed scores of marathons in fancy dress, and even beat Belinda Neild doesn't do anyt
input.evidence_text: Salford 'If my dad was alive he'd be laughing his head off going 'yes, get in!'' Teacher Belinda Neild took up running after the death of her mum on the eve of her wedding day. She has since completed scores of marathons in fancy dress, and even beat Belinda Neild doesn't do anything by halves. And recently, it has seen the 49-year-old has become a familiar face across Manchester city centre Her journey started when her mother died just one day before her wedding in 2011. The tragedy inspired her to run her first 10k dressed as a sumo wrestler Completing her feat, she raised £200 for the hospice that had cared for her mum and her dad who passed away before Christmas. But her journey didn't stop there Now, Belinda, from Swinton , has secured herself a place in the Guinness Book of Records for taking part in marathons in fancy dress. And is now seen across the region on banners, donning a bee outfit, to advertise the Great Manchester Run along Deansgate This year, she is embarking on her toughest feat yet - a 100-mile endeavour to raise vital funds for Moya Cole Hospice. The charity cared for her mother and father-in-law before they died, with Belinda also running for other relatives and friends she has lost to cancer

title: Another £1m dished out, but is the rest of Stockport getting its fair share?

input.lead: Stockport Another £1m dished out, but is the rest of Stockport getting its fair share?
input.summary: Stockport Another £1m dished out, but is the rest of Stockport getting its fair share? "We have worked extensively to maximise the minimal resources we have" Comments News Edward Barnes Wirral Local Democracy Reporter 07:09, 31 May 2026 View 2 Images A row has broken out over whe
input.evidence_text: Stockport Another £1m dished out, but is the rest of Stockport getting its fair share? "We have worked extensively to maximise the minimal resources we have" Comments News Edward Barnes Wirral Local Democracy Reporter 07:09, 31 May 2026 View 2 Images A row has broken out over whether some parts of Stockport are getting their ‘fair share’. It comes after Bramall Hall was awarded another £1m Bramall Hall, a Grade I-listed heritage site, is one of Stockport’s most treasured historical buildings. The manor house in Bramhall is largely Tudor in design with its oldest parts dating back to the 14th century The hall has been undergoing renovations to preserve the building going forward after £1.6m was awarded in 2024 to fix long-term issues in the roof. Now Stockport Council has announced an extra £1m has been awarded by the UK Government from the Museum Estate and Development Fund Coun Dan Oliver, the Cabinet Member responsible for culture at Stockport Council, said: “This funding is a big boost in helping us protect and enhance it for future generations "The funding will support vital upgrades to this nationally significant building, helping to protect its historic fabric while improving access for visitors, families and school groups

title: How police shut down Manchester's most infamous pub

input.lead: True crime series How police shut down Manchester's most infamous pub The Pepperhill took its name from a farm which featured in an Elizabeth Gaskell novel, but it would become better known for its criminal rather than literary associations View 3 Ima News Damon Wilkinson Reporte
input.summary: True crime series How police shut down Manchester's most infamous pub The Pepperhill took its name from a farm which featured in an Elizabeth Gaskell novel, but it would become better known for its criminal rather than literary associations View 3 Ima News Damon Wilkinson Reporte
input.evidence_text: True crime series How police shut down Manchester's most infamous pub The Pepperhill took its name from a farm which featured in an Elizabeth Gaskell novel, but it would become better known for its criminal rather than literary associations View 3 Ima News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 16:55, 30 May 2026 It gave its name to one of Manchester' s most feared gangs. But the Pepperhill pub's place in underworld infamy came about through sheer coincidence "A mate's sister used to live in Alexandra Park estate, on the east side of it, and that is where we used to hang out," original Pepperhill Mob member Anthony Stevens once said. "We used to stash guns and drugs at her house "There was a pub across the road called the Pepperhill. That’s where we used to drink, so people trying to describe that group of lads, instead of going through their names, they would just go, 'the Pepperhill lot' "That is how that came about. It wasn’t like we would sit there and say, 'We need a name, what do we call our gang?'" Opened in 1981, the Pepperhill was a classic flat-roofed estate pub tucked away on a side street. It took its name from Pepper Hill Farm, which stood on the area in the 1800s and featured in Elizabeth Gaskell's novel Mary Barton

The 87-year-old, who grew up in Wigan and Bolton , joined protesters to highlight the laws in 29 Commonwealth countries where same- They include six countries where LGBTQ+ people face life imprisonment, while Uganda and Nigeria still carry the death penalty. Sir Ian discussed how widespread banning of same-sex relationships was introduced by the British Empire - but while many countries have repealed the arch "When Britain had an empire, it introduced into countries far away from here the laws that we had in this country, including the laws against gay people," said Sir Ian. "Those laws have quite rightly been repealed in this country where gay people live freely and openly alongside "Unfortunately half of the Commonwealth countries stick with those old laws, and our plea to them today is to live up to the standards and declaration of the Commonwealth and repeal them." Sir Ian spoke out ahead of a demonstration billed the 'Commonwealth Walk of Shame', which w After being addressed by Sir Ian, along with Nigerian activist Babatunde Akanwale Osunseyi, the marchers set off to protest outside the high commissions of eight Commonwealth countries that criminalise LGBTQ+ people. "I told them 'that's a sign from God'. I told them to go home." They didn't go home. Uways Hussain and Usmon Mahmood, sucking nitrous oxide from balloons, continued motoring along south Manchester streets at speeds of up to 139mph Later that night, an innocent man was killed. The scene of the horror crash that claimed Sylvester Abayomi's life was horrific. Images of the wrecked cars appalled those in court as Hussain and Mahmood faced justice months later The Manchester Evening News spoke to people at the scene on Kingsway in Burnage in the wake of the tragedy. One man said he knew the pair. They were on the stretch as takeaways there stay open late on Ramadan, he said He revealed he had begged them to go home when he found out they were up to. "They've been driving fast late at night," he said

input.evidence_text: Health Heartbroken at losing her daughter, she made a choice that will change lives After suffering the devastating loss of her baby daughter, Lauren Caulfield became decided to train in midwifery at the University of Manchester News Bethan Finighan S Lauren Caulfield's daughter Grace was stillborn nearly fear years ago. It's tragedy that forged her career path and could change care for grieving families across the NHS Now in her third and final year studying midwifery at the University of Manchester , the 29-year-old has turned her heartbreak into a mission to improve bereavement care She said her own experiences with grief following the tragic loss of her daughter exposed major gaps in maternity bereavement training. And after choosing to study midwifery in a bid to make the changes she wishes she had seen, she noticed that bereavement education is often 'ove This led her to create an app designed to help student midwives and maternity staff provide compassionate care to anyone experiencing pregnancy or baby loss Named 'Gracefully' after her daughter, the app acts as a 'one-stop shop' for bereavement care guidance, covering everything from breaking bad news to supporting parents during future pregnancies

input.evidence_text: Salford 'If my dad was alive he'd be laughing his head off going 'yes, get in!'' Teacher Belinda Neild took up running after the death of her mum on the eve of her wedding day. She has since completed scores of marathons in fancy dress, and even beat Belinda Neild doesn't do anything by halves. And recently, it has seen the 49-year-old has become a familiar face across Manchester city centre Her journey started when her mother died just one day before her wedding in 2011. The tragedy inspired her to run her first 10k dressed as a sumo wrestler Completing her feat, she raised £200 for the hospice that had cared for her mum and her dad who passed away before Christmas. But her journey didn't stop there Now, Belinda, from Swinton , has secured herself a place in the Guinness Book of Records for taking part in marathons in fancy dress. And is now seen across the region on banners, donning a bee outfit, to advertise the Great Manchester Run along Deansgate This year, she is embarking on her toughest feat yet - a 100-mile endeavour to raise vital funds for Moya Cole Hospice. The charity cared for her mother and father-in-law before they died, with Belinda also running for other relatives and friends she has lost to cancer

"We have worked extensively to maximise the minimal resources we have" Comments News Edward Barnes Wirral Local Democracy Reporter 07:09, 31 May 2026 View 2 Images A row has broken out over whether some parts of Stockport are getting their ‘fair share’. It comes after Bramall Hall was awarded another £1m Bramall Hall, a Grade I-listed heritage site, is one of Stockport’s most treasured historical buildings. The manor house in Bramhall is largely Tudor in design with its oldest parts dating back to the 14th century The hall has been undergoing renovations to preserve the building going forward after £1.6m was awarded in 2024 to fix long-term issues in the roof. Now Stockport Council has announced an extra £1m has been awarded by the UK Government from the Museum Estate and Development Fund Coun Dan Oliver, the Cabinet Member responsible for culture at Stockport Council, said: “This funding is a big boost in helping us protect and enhance it for future generations "The funding will support vital upgrades to this nationally significant building, helping to protect its historic fabric while improving access for visitors, families and school groups

title: How police shut down Manchester's most infamous pub

input.lead: True crime series How police shut down Manchester's most infamous pub The Pepperhill took its name from a farm which featured in an Elizabeth Gaskell novel, but it would become better known for its criminal rather than literary associations View 3 Ima News Damon Wilkinson Reporte
input.summary: True crime series How police shut down Manchester's most infamous pub The Pepperhill took its name from a farm which featured in an Elizabeth Gaskell novel, but it would become better known for its criminal rather than literary associations View 3 Ima News Damon Wilkinson Reporte
input.evidence_text: True crime series How police shut down Manchester's most infamous pub The Pepperhill took its name from a farm which featured in an Elizabeth Gaskell novel, but it would become better known for its criminal rather than literary associations View 3 Ima News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 16:55, 30 May 2026 It gave its name to one of Manchester' s most feared gangs. But the Pepperhill pub's place in underworld infamy came about through sheer coincidence "A mate's sister used to live in Alexandra Park estate, on the east side of it, and that is where we used to hang out," original Pepperhill Mob member Anthony Stevens once said. "We used to stash guns and drugs at her house "There was a pub across the road called the Pepperhill. That’s where we used to drink, so people trying to describe that group of lads, instead of going through their names, they would just go, 'the Pepperhill lot' "That is how that came about. It wasn’t like we would sit there and say, 'We need a name, what do we call our gang?'" Opened in 1981, the Pepperhill was a classic flat-roofed estate pub tucked away on a side street. It took its name from Pepper Hill Farm, which stood on the area in the 1800s and featured in Elizabeth Gaskell's novel Mary Barton

input.evidence_text: Salford 'If my dad was alive he'd be laughing his head off going 'yes, get in!'' Teacher Belinda Neild took up running after the death of her mum on the eve of her wedding day. She has since completed scores of marathons in fancy dress, and even beat Belinda Neild doesn't do anything by halves. And recently, it has seen the 49-year-old has become a familiar face across Manchester city centre Her journey started when her mother died just one day before her wedding in 2011. The tragedy inspired her to run her first 10k dressed as a sumo wrestler Completing her feat, she raised £200 for the hospice that had cared for her mum and her dad who passed away before Christmas. But her journey didn't stop there Now, Belinda, from Swinton , has secured herself a place in the Guinness Book of Records for taking part in marathons in fancy dress. And is now seen across the region on banners, donning a bee outfit, to advertise the Great Manchester Run along Deansgate This year, she is embarking on her toughest feat yet - a 100-mile endeavour to raise vital funds for Moya Cole Hospice. The charity cared for her mother and father-in-law before they died, with Belinda also running for other relatives and friends she has lost to cancer

input.evidence_text: Stockport Another £1m dished out, but is the rest of Stockport getting its fair share? "We have worked extensively to maximise the minimal resources we have" Comments News Edward Barnes Wirral Local Democracy Reporter 07:09, 31 May 2026 View 2 Images A row has broken out over whether some parts of Stockport are getting their ‘fair share’. It comes after Bramall Hall was awarded another £1m Bramall Hall, a Grade I-listed heritage site, is one of Stockport’s most treasured historical buildings. The manor house in Bramhall is largely Tudor in design with its oldest parts dating back to the 14th century The hall has been undergoing renovations to preserve the building going forward after £1.6m was awarded in 2024 to fix long-term issues in the roof. Now Stockport Council has announced an extra £1m has been awarded by the UK Government from the Museum Estate and Development Fund Coun Dan Oliver, the Cabinet Member responsible for culture at Stockport Council, said: “This funding is a big boost in helping us protect and enhance it for future generations "The funding will support vital upgrades to this nationally significant building, helping to protect its historic fabric while improving access for visitors, families and school groups

But the Pepperhill pub's place in underworld infamy came about through sheer coincidence "A mate's sister used to live in Alexandra Park estate, on the east side of it, and that is where we used to hang out," original Pepperhill Mob member Anthony Stevens once said. "We used to stash guns and drugs at her house "There was a pub across the road called the Pepperhill. That’s where we used to drink, so people trying to describe that group of lads, instead of going through their names, they would just go, 'the Pepperhill lot' "That is how that came about. It wasn’t like we would sit there and say, 'We need a name, what do we call our gang?'" Opened in 1981, the Pepperhill was a classic flat-roofed estate pub tucked away on a side street. It took its name from Pepper Hill Farm, which stood on the area in the 1800s and featured in Elizabeth Gaskell's novel Mary Barton

The 87-year-old, who grew up in Wigan and Bolton , joined protesters to highlight the laws in 29 Commonwealth countries where same- They include six countries where LGBTQ+ people face life imprisonment, while Uganda and Nigeria still carry the death penalty. Sir Ian discussed how widespread banning of same-sex relationships was introduced by the British Empire - but while many countries have repealed the arch "When Britain had an empire, it introduced into countries far away from here the laws that we had in this country, including the laws against gay people," said Sir Ian. "Those laws have quite rightly been repealed in this country where gay people live freely and openly alongside "Unfortunately half of the Commonwealth countries stick with those old laws, and our plea to them today is to live up to the standards and declaration of the Commonwealth and repeal them." Its former use also reveals a tragic, unsolved murder and helps explai Long before it became Sacha's Hotel, the building was one of central Manchester's flagship retail destinations. Originally, it was built for C&A, an international clothing retailer C&A – or Coats and 'Ats as it was sometimes known – was the popular fashion giant founded in 1841 by German-Dutch brothers Clemens and August Brenninkmeijer. They used the initials of their first names to name the enterprise before it became a major UK retailer Clemens and August’s ancestors had long been successful traders in northern Holland, leaving the family farm in 1671 to sell their wares as travelling linen merchants. And it was in Mettingen in the 1830s that Clemens and August were apprenticed, little more than boys, to learn t By the start of the 20th century, the foundations had been laid for C&A to play a major role in making the latest fashions accessible and affordable. input.evidence_text: Bolton From four GCSEs and 'nothing' on a Bolton council estate to Frank Ocean and a £2m-a-year business Bolton-born Michael Mayren 'started with nothing' - and turned it into a successful model agency with an Oasis-inspired name View 4 Images Michael News Bethan Finighan Science and Innovation Writer 07:18, 30 May 2026 When Michael Mayren left school with four GCSEs and no A-levels, he never imagined he would one day run a Manchester business turning over more than £2m a year Growing up on a council estate in Horwich with a young single mum, the 40-year-old says university "wasn't something I thought I'd be capable of". But nearly a decade after launching Brother Models from his bedroom with just a laptop, the agency now works with global fashion gian Michael, who now lives in Chorley, said he "didn't really intend" to set up a major model agency - and instead thought he was embarking on a "side hustle". But over the years, its models and creators have also appeared in campaigns for Chanel and Primark, as well as music videos View 4 Images Michael Mayren shoots for fashion magazines like GQ Style, ID and Man About Town (Image: Michael Mayren) He told the Manchester Evening News : "From being pretty young, I was never a kid that could focus or concentrate on that much, but I was always pretty creative

Instead, it's a daily r The 39-year-old single mum Gemma, who has a two year-old daughter, was made homeless just six months ago. Originally from Shaw, the council offered her a flat in the Coldhurst area of Oldham “It’s a two bed flat but on the second floor with no lift, so getting up and down with the shopping and a little one is a challenge” she tells the Manchester Evening News as she stands holding her daughter’s hand outside a parade of shops north of the town centre “The area is actually quite quiet. I have not seen any drama in the time I’ve been here. It’s just very unclean to look at.” But, she adds, the area’s appearance is currently the least of her worries: “I work full-time, but paying nursery fees and the general cost of living, it just has a massive impact

Rubble quickly piled up on the ground around it in what was dubbed by devastated locals a 'Mancunian tragedy.' A cordon was put in place as structural experts were drafted in And it has now been fully demolished, Trafford Council confirmed this evening (Friday). input.summary: Courts Sick confessions of a pervert looking for a 'thrill' William Kidd has been jailed News Chris Slater Senior Reporter 18:26, 29 May 2026 View 2 Images William Kidd (Image: Cheshire Police) A 'predator' who violated women at shops across Greater Manchester said he 'did it for
input.evidence_text: Courts Sick confessions of a pervert looking for a 'thrill' William Kidd has been jailed News Chris Slater Senior Reporter 18:26, 29 May 2026 View 2 Images William Kidd (Image: Cheshire Police) A 'predator' who violated women at shops across Greater Manchester said he 'did it for a sexual thrill'. William Kidd, 27, from Stockport, was caught four times on CCTV following women at stores before spraying a liquid at them from a bottle He aimed at their hair or backs, with his victims often unaware what had happened, Cheshire Police said. Forensic analysis later revealed the substance was Kidd's semen He struck at shops in Stockport and Denton in neighbouring Tameside - as well as Macclesfield, Handforth and Stanley Green in Cheshire - between May and September last year When confronted by a security guard he said: "I'm a sex addict." He told another: "I did it to get a sexual thrill." Police said Kidd was once seen following a woman, who was shopping with her five-year-old daughter, and filming her He was identified following a public appeal and arrested last October. A search of his phone uncovered disturbing footage, including a video following a woman pushing a child in a shopping trolley while he filmed up her skirt

input.lead: Wednesday 6 May 2026 at 12:11pm Newly restored locomotive and carriages covered in graffiti at the East Lancashire Railway based at Bury.
input.summary: Wednesday 6 May 2026 at 12:11pm Newly restored locomotive and carriages covered in graffiti at the East Lancashire Railway based at Bury. Credit: East Lancashire Railway
input.evidence_text: Wednesday 6 May 2026 at 12:11pm Newly restored locomotive and carriages covered in graffiti at the East Lancashire Railway based at Bury. Credit: East Lancashire Railway. The East Lancashire Railway says it has again been the victim of a 'deliberate' and 'disgraceful' act of vand Police are investigating after a locomotive and several coaches from the Inter City 125 heritage set were targeted in what the railway describe as a mindless attack, with extensive graffiti across the rolling stock In a statement the East Lancashire railway said "This was not opportunistic behaviour — it was a calculated act of destruction against irreplaceable heritage assets cared for by volunteers "Once discovered, volunteers immediately stepped in and worked tirelessly for several hours using specialist cleaning materials to remove the graffiti before it could permanently set "Their swift action prevented what could have been significant and lasting damage, although further polishing and restoration work is still required."

input.evidence_text: Tuesday 21 April 2026 at 4:58pm Drivers at Greater Manchester Mental Health Trust will begin three further weeks of strikes. Credit: Jordan Pettitt/PA Mental health staff are set to strike over pay, UNISON has confirmed Drivers at Greater Manchester Mental Health Trust will begin three further weeks of strikes after bosses ignored their own report and failed to improve staff pay, the union reports The drivers - who transport samples, medication, linen and food across Greater Manchester – say they have already completed a job evaluation that found their roles should be upgraded to a higher pay grade But the union says bosses have not acted on the findings and have instead asked for a second opinion from another NHS trust. It says 11 drivers have been left thousands of pounds out of pocket UNISON North West regional organiser Paddy Cleary said: "Trust bosses have failed to act in time and workers have been left with no choice but to strike. Nobody wants to take this step, but staff are underpaid for the vital work they do – as the trust’s own report makes clear "The job evaluation process was followed correctly. These staff should be on a higher rate of pay. Instead of putting this right, senior managers have chosen to delay the inevitable by seeking unnecessary advice

title: Two arrested after investigation into City Centre illegal gambling - About Manchester

input.summary: Two people have been arrested as part of a proactive investigation into reports of an illegal gambling premises operating in the city centre. Police from the City of Manchester team – working alongside colleagues from the Gambling Commission and Manchester City Council Licensing
input.evidence_text: Two people have been arrested as part of a proactive investigation into reports of an illegal gambling premises operating in the city centre Police from the City of Manchester team – working alongside colleagues from the Gambling Commission and Manchester City Council Licensing Team – visited a premises on Chester Road as part of partnership enforcement activity Two people – one man and one woman aged 33 and 66– were arrested on suspicion of two offences under the Gambling Act 2005 and Licensing Act 2003 respectively PC Nial Vivian, said: “It is important that all businesses adhere to the laws put in place, and which is why we took action in the city centre yesterday “Illegal gambling can cause many issues – from disputes about cash to money laundering – and it is important that we shut them down whenever we come across them Sue Young, Gambling Commission Executive Director of Operations, said: “Tackling all forms of illegal gambling is a focus for the Commission so we were keen to work in partnership with Greater Manchester Police and Manchester City Council on this operation.”

input.summary: William Kidd, 27, was convicted of 25 offences and sentenced to four years imprisonment in a hearing at Manchester Minshull Street Crown Court At a previous hearing, he pleaded not guilty to 11 counts of sexual assault, eight counts of filming women underneath their clothing and
input.evidence_text: William Kidd, 27, was convicted of 25 offences and sentenced to four years imprisonment in a hearing at Manchester Minshull Street Crown Court At a previous hearing, he pleaded not guilty to 11 counts of sexual assault, eight counts of filming women underneath their clothing and one count of possession of cannabis - but he changed his pleas to guilty at the hearing on Friday He previously pleaded guilty to five counts of voyeurism The court heard Kidd, of no fixed abode, committed a number of offences against women in shops in the Macclesfield and Handforth areas of Cheshire and the Denton area of Greater Manchester from May to September 2025 He was caught on CCTV following a number of women around various shops before spraying liquid from a bottle onto them, without their knowledge. He was also recorded filming some women from behind, underneath their clothing When asked why he was spraying the liquid at women, he told one security guard, "I'm a sex addict", and another, "I did it to get a sexual thrill"

title: Building momentum: Another record year for affordable housing in Manchester

input.lead: City Council has achieved another record year for building new affordable homes, four years into its ambitious 10-year housing strategy for the city
input.summary: City Council has achieved another record year for building new affordable homes, four years into its ambitious 10-year housing strategy for the city
input.evidence_text: City Council has achieved another record year for building new affordable homes, four years into its ambitious 10-year housing strategy for the city Almost 1,000 of the 4,766 new homes completed across the city in 2025/26 were affordable, with 439 of these 901 affordable homes available for social rent This represents the most successful year since the mid-1990s for social and affordable home building in the city but there is much more to come as momentum accelerates There are around 1,450 further council, social and genuinely affordable homes already on site now with around 2,000 more such homes ready to start, 2026/27 looks set to be another bumper year The Council set out its 10-year housing strategy in 2022 with a bold commitment to help deliver 36,000 homes across across all tenures and areas of the city with at least 10,000 of these social, council or genuinely affordable homes, and at least 3,000 of these in the city centre With 2,430 such affordable homes already completed and a strong pipeline of future projects already either under construction, with planning permission and being readied for site, or on the horizon, there is every confidence that these targets will be met or exceeded

title: Huge Medieval event to celebrate Rochdale's historic roots - Rochdale Borough Council

input.lead: A massive Medieval fair, with music, activities, combat displays and more, is set to take over Rochdale town centre this June
input.summary: A massive Medieval fair, with music, activities, combat displays and more, is set to take over Rochdale town centre this June
input.evidence_text: A massive Medieval fair, with music, activities, combat displays and more, is set to take over Rochdale town centre this June Located in Rochdale Town Hall, Town Hall Square and the newly restored Broadfield Park Slopes, Rochdale Medieval Fayre, named in a nod to the historic word for fair, will transport visitors back in time to the Medieval period where they will see history burst into colourful life Taking place over the weekend of Saturday, 6–Sunday, 7 June 2026, visitors can expect combat displays, live cookery demonstrations and reenactments of daily life from the period, which was from 1066 to around 1485 Around 80 actors from 12th century living history group, Historia Normannis, will be showcasing life as it was hundreds of years ago, including re-enactments of Medieval law courts A full Medieval encampment will be set up with Medieval fashion shows and music from the period, while visitors can try their hand at coin striking and archery sessions The event, which is being funded by the National Lottery Heritage Fund as part of the Broadfield Park Slopes restoration project, will shine a spotlight on Rochdale town centre’s rich historic roots

input.lead: Wastewater from a sewer in Rochdale is set to supply 28GWh of low-cost, low-carbon heating to the town’s hospital, schools, leisure centre and homes

input.evidence_text: Wastewater from a sewer in Rochdale is set to supply 28GWh of low-cost, low-carbon heating to the town’s hospital, schools, leisure centre and homes Supported by £1 million from the Government’s Green Heat Network Fund, the project will link public buildings in the town centre, with work due to start in 2029 as part of Rochdale’s ongoing town centre regeneration Heat networks are expected to play an important role in cutting heating emissions, which made up more than a third of the borough’s emissions in 2023. Whilst this technology is relatively new in the UK, wastewater heat recovery is already used at over 500 sites worldwide 2 heat sources were assessed, and a town centre sewer source heat pump was chosen as the best option, which extracts the thermal energy found in wastewater using a combination of heat exchangers and a heat pump, with an energy centre planned for the current Mecca Bingo car park n By removing the need for individual heat pumps, boilers and hot water tanks in buildings, the network would provide cheaper, cleaner heating, with lower energy bills and carbon emissions with heating and hot water delivered through a heat exchanger about the size of a small gas b Councillor Tricia Ayrton, deputy leader of the council and cabinet member for climate change and environment, said

input.summary: Bolton’s Queen’s Park celebrates its one hundred and sixtieth birthday this Saturday with music, dance and activities
input.evidence_text: Bolton’s Queen’s Park celebrates its one hundred and sixtieth birthday this Saturday with music, dance and activities Today’s visitors find a park that, thanks to major investment by the council, is back to its best The ceremonial opening of the gates to the new park on Thursday, May 24, 1866, was a moment of huge celebration and pride for the town Many shops shut for the day and workers were given time off to join the festivities, which included a procession from the town centre out to the park The park quickly became the pride of Bolton and was much loved by generations of local people looking for fresh air, nature and relaxation Anyone who was there on that first day would still recognise much of the park today

title: Health column: Smoke Free Generation - A rare landmark moment for public health

input.lead: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
input.summary: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
input.evidence_text: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tobacco to anyone born on, or after, 1st January 2009 - and, in doing so, creating the UK’s first ‘smoke‑free generation’ Make no mistake that this is one of the most significant public health reforms in a generation, and one that sends a clear signal: preventing harm before it begins matters Smoking remains the leading preventable cause of illness and early death in the UK. It significantly increases a person’s risk of cancer, heart disease and stroke, places huge pressure on the NHS, and continues to widen the gap in life expectancy across our communities The new law takes a bold, long‑term approach to breaking that cycle by ensuring today’s children never legally start smoking at all We know that information, advice and individual willpower can only go so far. Real, lasting change happens when policy and environments support people to make healthier choices

title: Safer knife replacement scheme rolls out across Wigan Borough

input.lead: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign
input.summary: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign
input.evidence_text: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign - set up by Leanne Lucas, a survivor of the Southport attack Nearly 20 council and partner buildings have already made the switch to the knives which feature a rounded-ended tip, as well as many residential homes taking up the pledge and making the swap Leanne Lucas said: “Replacing pointed tip knives is a simple but important step in reducing risk and preventing harm “Rounded tips make a real difference; the pointed tip creates the pressure point and increases the potential for serious injury “We also must recognise that harm is often about access and opportunity. When everyday items are easily available, they can be used in moments of crisis, impulse or vulnerability, so making small changes like this helps reduce that immediate risk

title: Marina Abramović: Balkan Erotic Epic | Aviva Studios

input.lead: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović .
input.summary: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance a
input.evidence_text: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance artist, Abramović’s transformative work has reached millions of people – from opening the Pyramid Stage at Glastonbury Festival t A four-hour ritual where ancient myth meets performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic explores the eroticism, spirituality and traditions of Abramović’s homeland through 13 visceral scenes Brought to life by a cast of over 70 performers, dancers, musicians and singers, the experience unfolds across Aviva Studios. Audiences are free to choose their own path – with pop-up encounters punctuating the action, bringing bodies together in dance, song and ritual Watch Fertility Rite , a fevered ritual where bodies writhe against the ground in a desperate call for fertility. Witness Massaging the Breast where women gesticulate over graves to awaken the earth. In Scaring the Gods , women bare themselves to the sky to banish storms. Listen Unafraid to push the boundaries of eroticism and performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic asks its audience a timeless question: What are our bodies truly for?

title: RNCM Music Industry Panel

input.lead: Where does music education sit within the real world of gigs, radio, and industry work?
input.summary: Where does music education sit within the real world of gigs, radio, and industry work? This panel brings together artists and industry professionals working across Manchester’s grassroots scene and beyond
input.evidence_text: Where does music education sit within the real world of gigs, radio, and industry work? This panel brings together artists and industry professionals working across Manchester’s grassroots scene and beyond. It opens up conversations around how careers begin and build, and the wider context that supports that journey alongside formal study Rather than focusing only on what happens within the classroom, this session looks at the broader activity that happens alongside classroom learning. In particular, it explores the networks, spaces, opportunities, and experiences that shape how people find their way into music RNCM Alumni : independent artists building momentum through releases and live performance Deb Grant BBC6Music : broadcaster championing new music and emerging artists David Agnew Soundroots : working across grassroots venues, funding, development and touring The session will explore how the Manchester scene operates, where early momentum comes from, and how education, industry and grassroots activity intersect in practice This event is open to prospective students, current students, and anyone interested in how music scenes and careers develop

One of the largest Victorian walled gardens in the UK, it showcases a range of growing techniques and edible plants There’s also the spectacular Paradise Garden showcasing exotic planting inspired by Asiatic and Mediterranean gardens. The orchard and nearby bee and butterfly garden, have been designed to attract wildlife The Peel Learning Garden, supported by The Peel Group, will help schoolchildren learn how plants affect our lives and the planet, whilst the Community Wellbeing Garden provides a space for people to garden, socialise and relax

input.evidence_text: As Manchester Digital reaches its 25th year, it has today announced details of its new Manchester Digital Festival for 2027. The festival runs from February 8-12 across various venues in the city region, anchored by a dedicated festival hub for the first time. The new festival is "Greater Manchester's tech story has changed," said Katie Gallagher, Managing Director of Manchester Digital. "We're no longer making the case that this region matters - that case is made. Since the Digital Skills Festival was launched, the Manchester tech economy has doubled in “This new festival reflects where the sector actually is: broader, more confident and increasingly setting the national conversation rather than contributing to it. 25 years on from when Manchester Digital started, we wanted to mark it with something that belongs to the whole sec “We’re looking at what the tech sector in Manchester and the wider North West region could look like in another 25 years and what we collectively need to do as an industry to shape that future right now.” The skills agenda remains central to the festival but the whole of Manchester’s tech sector is reflected in the new-look Manchester Digital Festival. input.evidence_text: Digital Infrastructure North 2026 (DIN26) will take place on 13 May 2026, bringing together senior leaders from government, infrastructure operators, technology companies and research organisations to help shape the next phase of the UK’s digital infrastructure development, with DIN26 is a discussion-led forum rather than a commercial conference. It is designed to create a high-trust environment for open, practical dialogue between those responsible for building, operating and regulating the infrastructure that underpins the UK’s digital economy. Attenda The forum is being convened by Manchester Digital , Cooperative Network Infrastructure , Greater Manchester Combined Authority and LINX , reflecting a shared commitment to strengthening the UK’s digital foundations and ensuring the benefits of growth are felt across the country The UK is entering a critical phase in the development of its digital infrastructure. Demand for compute capacity is accelerating rapidly as large language models and other data-intensive technologies become central to the digital economy At the same time, the country’s infrastructure remains heavily concentrated in London and the South East, where power capacity is increasingly constrained. Meanwhile, renewable energy generation in the North is often curtailed because it cannot be easily transported to where dema Northern cities, including Manchester, are home to major research institutions, established digital industries and fast-growing AI and innovation ecosystems. The square itself ha From here, the tour takes you through the city’s heart, down the colourful streets of the Gay Village and the calming Sackville Gardens. The Medieval Quarter is the perfect place to soak in all that history. It is here where you will see the oldest public library in the English-speaking world, Chetham’s Library . input.evidence_text: What gets to become history, and who decides? 4-6 June 2026 Additional information Always double check opening hours with the venue before making a special visit 10 editions in, and Manchester Histories Festival is still finding stories that official records have skirted over This city’s always been good at telling its own tale. Sometimes so good, in fact, that history tips towards mythology. Manchester Histories Festival has spent a decade grounding it, often by handing the storytelling back to the people The clearest expression of that this year is the Threads of a City: The Manchester Tapestry Project – a large-scale collaborative textile work spanning Manchester’s identity from its incorporation in 1853 to the present. Community groups have spent months researching the themes – Open to anyone aged 15 and over, stitching workshops run across all three days of the festival, inviting the public to add their own hand to the work before it’s displayed at Manchester Town Hall

input.evidence_text: The South Manchester Food Festival 2026: A Springtime Feast – Wythenshawe Park & Gardens, Sat 16 May 2026 - Sun 17 May 2026 - Manchester's Springtime Feast is returning to Wythenshawe Park & Gardens for its BIGGEST celebration to date! s Springtime Feast is returning to Wythenshawe Park & Gardens for its BIGGEST celebration to date! After an incredible 2025 event, we’re heading to the back to the stunning fields of Wythenshawe Park & Gardens for a weekend jam-packed with brand-new production, eccentric live entertainment, and an abundance of culinary delights 🌳 Alongside the cornerstone classics, you can expect Mark the date, grab your family and friends, and lock yourselves in for Manchester's ultimate foodie celebration this Spring. Last year tickets were snapped up faster than Yorkshire puds at a Sunday Roast... don't hang about - get yours before they're all gone! Join thousands of Stroll through the peaceful green fields of Wythenshawe Park and follow the tempting scents of world flavours as they lead you on a global adventure courtesy of some of the UK’s best street food traders. input.evidence_text: Listing was last Updated on 21 February 2024 The Bolton Car Boot Sale is held at the Macron / Reebok football stadium every Sunday from February until December. The carboot sale opens at 6.00 AM for sellers and 7.00 AM for buyers. The car boot sale can operate in all weather conditions due to having both indoor and outdoor The sale attracts over 300 sellers and thousands of buyers every week. It allows sellers to empty their sheds, garages and lofts and make money from their old items It’s recommended that sellers bring the exact change to minimize queueing time The admission fee is £1 per car from 7.00 AM. The car boot sale is pet-friendly and offers a 5-star catering service on-site. Male and female toilets are available on-site for convenience. Other facilities include kids’ rides and bouncy castles, doughnuts & coffee, a meat auction The Bolton Car Boot Sale is an excellent opportunity for buyers and sellers alike.
